sections.heading: Algorithm
sections.body: Depth First Search explores as far as possible down one branch of the search tree before backtracking to try the next alternative. It dives deep first, rather than expanding broadly level by level like BFS.

sections.heading: Stack Implementation
sections.body: DFS is implemented using a LIFO (last-in, first-out) stack for its frontier — the most recently generated node is expanded next, which naturally produces the "go deep, then backtrack" behavior.

sections.heading: Recursive DFS
sections.body: DFS is often implemented recursively, where a function calls itself on each child node — the call stack itself acts as the LIFO structure implicitly, without needing an explicit stack data structure in the code.

sections.heading: Properties
sections.body: DFS is not complete in infinite state spaces (it can get stuck going down one infinite branch and never backtrack to try others) and is not optimal even when it does find a solution (the first goal found may not be the cheapest one).

sections.heading: Applications
sections.body: DFS is useful when memory is limited, when any solution (not necessarily the best) is acceptable, or for tasks like topological sorting, cycle detection, and exhaustively exploring all possibilities in puzzles like maze generation/solving.

sections.heading: Complexity Analysis
sections.body: DFS's time complexity is O(b^m), where m is the maximum depth of the tree (which can be much worse than BFS's d if m is large or infinite). Its key advantage is space complexity of only O(bm) — linear in depth, since it only needs to remember the current path plus unexplored siblings, not the entire frontier.

quiz.question: DFS explores the search tree by:
options: Going as deep as possible down one branch before backtracking

quiz.question: What is DFS's key space-complexity advantage over BFS?
options: DFS uses no memory at all
DFS's space complexity is linear in depth (O(bm)), much lower than BFS's exponential O(b^d)
DFS always uses more memory than BFS

quiz.question: Is DFS guaranteed to find the optimal (cheapest) solution?
options: No — the first goal it finds may not be the cheapest
